title: Klinefelter Syndrome
oneLiner: 47,XXY chromosomal disorder in males causing primary hypogonadism, tall stature, small testes, and infertility; often missed until puberty or adulthood.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 16-year-old male presents for a routine school check. He is noticeably tall and thin, with long legs and a narrow chest. His parents mention he has always struggled with reading and was placed in a special class for dyslexia. On examination, you find scant chest and facial hair, small firm testes (2–3 ml bilaterally), and mild breast tissue (gynecomastia). His voice is high-pitched and he has difficulty concentrating during the consultation. Baseline bloods show elevated FSH and LH, low-normal testosterone, and haemoglobin in the low range for his age.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern clue | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| Tall, thin stature with eunuchoid proportions (arm span > height, increased floor-to-pubis length) | Oestrogen deficiency from low testosterone causes delayed epiphyseal fusion, leading to continued linear growth |
| Small, firm testes (<4 ml) | Sertoli cell dysfunction leads to hyalinisation of seminiferous tubules; testicular volume is a cardinal sign |
| Gynecomastia | Increased oestrogen-to-testosterone ratio due to aromatase activity on residual androgens, plus altered SHBG |
| Learning difficulties (especially language-based) | Extra X chromosome affects frontal and temporal lobe development, impairing verbal fluency and executive function |
| High FSH/LH with low/normal testosterone | Primary testicular failure – Leydig and Sertoli cells are responsive to gonadotropins but cannot produce adequate T or inhibin |
| Mild anaemia (Hb ~110–120 g/L) | Low testosterone reduces erythropoietin drive (androgen effect on bone marrow) |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Mosaic Klinefelter (46,XY/47,XXY)** – Near-normal testicular size, partial spermatogenesis, may father children; present with infertility only in adulthood. Often misdiagnosed as idiopathic hypogonadism.
2. **Infantile hypotonia and cryptorchidism** – In neonates, poor feeding, undescended testes, and hypermobile joints may be dismissed as “floppy baby” – but karyotype reveals 47,XXY.
3. **Psychiatric or behavioural presentation** – A 20-year-old with anxiety, social withdrawal, and poor academic achievement who ends up in mental health services; on exam you find small testes and gynaecomastia – the “hidden Klinefelter” in psychiatry clinics.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Klinefelter syndrome is a genetic condition in which a male is born with at least one extra X chromosome (usually 47,XXY). This extra genetic material disrupts testicular development and function, leading to low testosterone, infertility, and a range of physical and cognitive traits that vary widely.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency**: 1 in 660 males – as common as Down syndrome, but diagnosed in only ~25% of cases (usually later in life).
- **Stakes**: Undiagnosed patients miss the window for testosterone therapy during puberty, leading to lifelong osteopenia, metabolic syndrome (diabetes, obesity), venous thromboembolism risk, and psychosocial distress.
- **Common pitfall**: Attributing tall stature and delayed puberty to “constitutional delay” without checking karyotype or gonadotropins; Klinefelter is the most common cause of primary hypogonadism.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of development:**

During meiosis, nondisjunction of the X chromosome in either the egg (60% maternal) or sperm (40% paternal) results in a sperm or egg carrying an extra X. When fertilisation occurs, the zygote has 47 chromosomes (XXY) instead of 46. The extra X is inactivated (Barr body) but a few genes escape silencing – particularly those on the pseudoautosomal regions and the X-linked short stature homeobox (SHOX) gene.

**Why each key sign happens:**

- **Testicular failure** → The extra X disrupts Sertoli cell differentiation and Leydig cell function. By puberty, seminiferous tubules hyalinise and become fibrotic → **small firm testes** (the pathognomonic feel). Sertoli cells fail to produce inhibin B → loss of negative feedback → **elevated FSH**. Leydig cells produce inadequate testosterone → **elevated LH** (compensatory). Result: azoospermia / severe oligospermia.

- **Tall stature and eunuchoid proportions** → SHOX gene overexpression (from two X chromosomes) accelerates linear growth. Low oestrogen (from low T conversion) delays epiphyseal fusion → legs keep growing. Arm span > height, and pubis-to-floor > crown-to-pubis.

- **Gynecomastia** → Low testosterone + preserved (or increased) oestrogen production from peripheral aromatisation of adrenal androgens → altered ratio → breast tissue growth. Also, SHBG is elevated by oestrogen → lowers free T further.

- **Learning difficulties** → The extra X affects brain regions responsible for language, verbal processing, and executive function. Patients often have dyslexia, impaired auditory processing, and reduced IQ (~10–15 points below siblings, but wide variability). Motor coordination is also affected.

- **Low bone mineral density** → Testosterone deficiency reduces osteoblastic activity → osteoporosis risk. Delayed puberty also reduces peak bone mass.

**Connecting pathophys to pattern recognition:**  
The classic vignette’s “small testes” and “high FSH” are direct consequences of Sertoli cell hyalinisation. The “learning difficulty” is a cortical effect of extra X. The “tall stature” is SHOX-driven. Each clue maps to a specific mechanistic step.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|---|---|---|
| **Chromosomal (most common)** | Nondisjunction during meiosis (paternal or maternal) | “Tall male with small testes; no family history” |
| **Mosaicism** | Post-zygotic mitotic error → 46,XY/47,XXY | “Less severe phenotype; may have normal testicular size” |
| **Additional X (rare)** | 48,XXXY or 49,XXXXY (increasing X count) | “More severe intellectual disability, dysmorphic face, radio-ulnar synostosis” |
| **Maternal age effect** | Slightly increased risk with older mothers | “Mother aged >40” (only 20% of cases, small effect) |

**Most common cause:** Sporadic nondisjunction in meiosis (no known trigger). No preventive strategy.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic confirmation:**

| Investigation | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Karyotype (peripheral blood)** | 47,XXY (or variant) | **Gold standard** – definitive diagnosis | One extra X in at least 10 cells; mosaic patterns show two cell lines. *Single finding confirms.* |
| **FSH, LH** | Elevated | Detect primary testicular failure | FSH > 10 IU/L, LH > 8 IU/L (in pubertal or adult male) suggests Sertoli/Leydig damage |
| **Testosterone (total + SHBG)** | Low-normal to low total; free T often low | Assess androgen status | Total T < 12 nmol/L; SHBG elevated → calculated free T even lower |
| **Semen analysis** | Azoospermia (or severe oligospermia) | Fertility assessment | Absent sperm in at least two samples; rarely, rare sperm present in mosaics |
| **Inhibin B** | Low or undetectable | Direct marker of Sertoli cell function | <15 pg/mL supports testicular atrophy |
| **Bone age X-ray (wrist)** | Delayed in adolescents | Assess growth potential and pubertal stage | If bone age < chronological age, consider timing of testosterone start |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms:**  
A **karyotype** showing 47,XXY is diagnostic. No other test alone confirms it – always order karyotype if clinical suspicion exists.

**Rule-out threshold:**  
If FSH and LH are normal and testicular volume > 12 ml, Klinefelter is very unlikely (except mosaics). Always check karyotype in infertile men with small testes regardless of gonadotropins.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**  
*No acute emergency.* Klinefelter is a chronic condition. If first diagnosed in a distressed adolescent, spend time counselling – explain that it is not their fault, and that treatments exist for testosterone and fertility.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – for newly diagnosed adolescent / adult:**

1. **Testosterone replacement therapy (TRT)** – Initiate if signs of hypogonadism (delayed puberty, low bone density, fatigue) and testosterone < 10 nmol/L.
   - **Testosterone enanthate** 100–250 mg IM every 2–4 weeks, titrate to trough T > 12 nmol/L.
   - *Alternative:* Testosterone gel 1% (e.g., Testogel) 50–100 mg daily – better for stable levels but risk of transfer.
   - **Why:** Restore virilisation, improve muscle mass, bone density, energy, libido, and mood. In adolescents, start at low dose (50 mg IM monthly) to avoid rapid growth spurt and early epiphyseal fusion.
   - **What NOT to do:** Do not use oral testosterone (hepatotoxic, unreliable). Do not start TRT before bone age > 12 years (risk of advanced bone age and short stature).

2. **Baseline metabolic screen** – Fasting glucose, lipids, HbA1c – because Klinefelter is associated with insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome.

3. **Refer to endocrinology** and **fertility specialist** (if desired) – discuss microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) success rate ~50% in non-mosaic adults; consider cryopreservation if any sperm found.

4. **Speech and language therapy referral** – if learning difficulties identified.

**Long-term:**

- **Continuous TRT** – lifelong unless contraindicated. Monitor haemoglobin, haematocrit (risk of polycythaemia), lipids, PSA (after age 45). Doses: Testosterone enanthate 100–250 mg IM q2–4weeks or gel 50–100 mg daily. *Aim for mid-normal free T.*
- **Osteoporosis monitoring** – DEXA scan at diagnosis, then q2–3 years if low T untreated; with TRT, monitor if risk factors persist.
- **Mental health support** – cognitive behavioural therapy for social anxiety, depression (common).
- **Gynecomastia management** – if cosmetic concern, consider surgery (subcutaneous mastectomy) after 2–3 years of TRT (some regression); tamoxifen 20 mg daily is second-line (risk of VTE).
- **Fertility** – micro-TESE with ICSI; consider donor sperm if unsuccessful.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**

- **Starting TRT too late** – after age 18, loss of bone mass may be irreversible.
- **Ignoring learning disability** – may lead to school dropout; early educational support improves outcomes.
- **Not checking karyotype in infertility workup** – the most common cause of non-obstructive azoospermia.
- **Assuming all tall, thin men have Klinefelter** – Marfan, homocystinuria, and eunuchoid habitus due to other hypogonadism should be excluded.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Klinefelter Syndrome | Kallmann Syndrome | Noonan Syndrome | Constitutional Delay of Puberty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pain | None | None | None | None |
| Exam | Tall, small testes, gynecomastia, eunuchoid | Anosmia, micropenis, cryptorchidism | Short stature, webbed neck, pectus excavatum, wide-spaced nipples | Short stature (normal height for bone age), small testes but normal for prepubertal |
| ECG | Normal | Normal | Often RVH (pulmonic stenosis) | Normal |
| Labs | High FSH/LH, low T, azoospermia | Low FSH/LH, low T (hypogonadotropic) | Normal FSH/LH (or mildly low), normal T for age | Low FSH/LH, low T (but appropriate for prepubertal stage) |
| Key differentiating test | Karyotype 47,XXY | MRI brain (absent olfactory bulbs) | Genetic panel (PTPN11, SOS1, RAF1) | Bone age < chronological age; eventual spontaneous pubertal progression |

**Note:** Always check karyotype before diagnosing constitutional delay if testicular volume < 4 ml at age 14 or if tall stature is present.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Untreated Klinefelter syndrome** has a cascade of late effects. The stems that test “what happens if untreated” often describe a man in his 30s–40s with unexplained fractures, diabetes, and a history of “never hitting puberty”.

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Osteoporosis** | Low-trauma fracture (hip, vertebral) | Bone density T-score < -2.5; short height loss | DEXA scan; start TRT + calcium/vitamin D; consider bisphosphonates if severe |
| **Metabolic syndrome / type 2 diabetes** | Obesity, hypertension, dyslipidaemia at age 30–40 | High BMI, high triglycerides, low HDL, impaired fasting glucose | Metformin 500 mg BD titrated; lifestyle; TRT improves insulin sensitivity |
| **Venous thromboembolism** | Unprovoked DVT/PE (especially if TRT started without monitoring) | Haematocrit > 0.54, leg swelling, dyspnoea | Stop TRT; anticoagulate; re-evaluate dose after VTE treatment |
| **Breast cancer** (risk ~20-fold increase over general male) | Painless breast lump, nipple retraction | Solid mass on mammogram; biopsy shows invasive carcinoma | Mastectomy + sentinel node; refer to oncology (treat as male breast cancer) |
| **Psychosocial disability** | Social isolation, unemployment, depression | Poor educational attainment, anxiety, low self-esteem | Cognitive behavioural therapy; vocational support; ensure adequate TRT |

**Late-presentation pattern (the “missed” patient):**  
A 45-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes, mild intellectual disability, and bilateral gynaecomastia. He has never fathered children. On examination, testes are 3 ml each. Karyotype shows 47,XXY. This patient has had untreated hypogonadism for 30+ years.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **Klinefelter: eXtra X, eXtra tall, eXtra small testes** – the three “X” clues.
2. **SST** – Small testes, Tall stature, Gynecomastia (the triad of Klinefelter).
3. **H-G-H** – High gonadotropins, Gynecomastia, Hypogonadism.

**Five high-yield clinical pearls:**

1. **If you see tall + small testes + learning difficulty, think Klinefelter before Marfan.** Marfan has arachnodactyly, lens dislocation, and normal testes.
2. **Normal testicular volume > 12 ml essentially rules out classic Klinefelter.** But mosaics can have normal volume – check karyotype in any infertile man.
3. **TRT does NOT restore fertility.** In fact, it suppresses any residual spermatogenesis. If fertility is desired, offer sperm retrieval before starting TRT, or store sperm if present.
4. **Breast cancer in a male?** Check for Klinefelter – the relative risk is 20-fold. All men with gynaecomastia should have a breast exam and mammogram if asymmetric or firm.
5. **Klinefelter patients have a higher risk of autoimmune diseases** (SLE, RA, type 1 diabetes) – possibly due to altered X-inactivation and immune dysregulation.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Attributing high FSH/LH to “testicular damage from mumps” – always karyotype.
- Not ordering karyotype in azoospermic men – it’s the single most cost-effective test.
- Starting oral testosterone (e.g., Testosterone undecanoate capsules) – poor bioavailability and hepatotoxic.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
Klinefelter syndrome itself is rarely an acute emergency. However, complications or TRT adverse effects can require escalation:

| Scenario | Threshold | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Suspected VTE (DVT/PE)** | Sudden onset leg swelling/pain, dyspnoea, hypoxia, haemoptysis | Call MET / crash team if PE with hypotension; start LMWH; check haematocrit |
| **Haematocrit > 0.54** on TRT | Asymptomatic or symptomatic (headache, confusion) | Pause TRT; consider phlebotomy if haematocrit > 0.56; review dose reduction |
| **Psychiatric crisis** | Suicidal ideation, severe depression, acute anxiety | Call liaison psychiatry; consider hospitalisation; reassess TRT adequacy |
| **Hypoglycaemia / diabetic ketoacidosis** | If known diabetes, vomiting, abdominal pain, high ketones | Call medical registrar; manage per DKA protocol |
| **GCS drop** | Traumatic fracture with possible hypercalcaemia (immobilisation + osteoporosis) | CT head; calcium levels; admit for monitoring |

**General escalation:**  
- NEWS2 score ≥ 5 → urgent medical review.  
- NEWS2 score ≥ 7 → immediate call to MET/crash team.  
- Any new focal neurological sign, seizure, or loss of consciousness → emergency CT and endocrine review.

---

*End of Study-Mode Entry*

## Klinefelter Syndrome
**One-liner:** 47,XXY chromosomal disorder in males causing primary hypogonadism, tall stature, small testes, and infertility; often missed until puberty or adulthood.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette:**  
A 16-year-old male presents for a routine school check. He is noticeably tall and thin, with long legs and a narrow chest. His parents mention he has always struggled with reading and was placed in a special class for dyslexia. On examination, you find scant chest and facial hair, small firm testes (2–3 ml bilaterally), and mild breast tissue (gynecomastia). His voice is high-pitched and he has difficulty concentrating during the consultation. Baseline bloods show elevated FSH and LH, low-normal testosterone, and haemoglobin in the low range for his age.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern clue | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| Tall, thin stature with eunuchoid proportions (arm span > height, increased floor-to-pubis length) | Oestrogen deficiency from low testosterone causes delayed epiphyseal fusion, leading to continued linear growth |
| Small, firm testes (<4 ml) | Sertoli cell dysfunction leads to hyalinisation of seminiferous tubules; testicular volume is a cardinal sign |
| Gynecomastia | Increased oestrogen-to-testosterone ratio due to aromatase activity on residual androgens, plus altered SHBG |
| Learning difficulties (especially language-based) | Extra X chromosome affects frontal and temporal lobe development, impairing verbal fluency and executive function |
| High FSH/LH with low/normal testosterone | Primary testicular failure – Leydig and Sertoli cells are responsive to gonadotropins but cannot produce adequate T or inhibin |
| Mild anaemia (Hb ~110–120 g/L) | Low testosterone reduces erythropoietin drive (androgen effect on bone marrow) |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Mosaic Klinefelter (46,XY/47,XXY)** – Near-normal testicular size, partial spermatogenesis, may father children; present with infertility only in adulthood. Often misdiagnosed as idiopathic hypogonadism.
2. **Infantile hypotonia and cryptorchidism** – In neonates, poor feeding, undescended testes, and hypermobile joints may be dismissed as “floppy baby” – but karyotype reveals 47,XXY.
3. **Psychiatric or behavioural presentation** – A 20-year-old with anxiety, social withdrawal, and poor academic achievement who ends up in mental health services; on exam you find small testes and gynaecomastia – the “hidden Klinefelter” in psychiatry clinics.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Plain-language definition:**  
Klinefelter syndrome is a genetic condition in which a male is born with at least one extra X chromosome (usually 47,XXY). This extra genetic material disrupts testicular development and function, leading to low testosterone, infertility, and a range of physical and cognitive traits that vary widely.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency**: 1 in 660 males – as common as Down syndrome, but diagnosed in only ~25% of cases (usually later in life).
- **Stakes**: Undiagnosed patients miss the window for testosterone therapy during puberty, leading to lifelong osteopenia, metabolic syndrome (diabetes, obesity), venous thromboembolism risk, and psychosocial distress.
- **Common pitfall**: Attributing tall stature and delayed puberty to “constitutional delay” without checking karyotype or gonadotropins; Klinefelter is the most common cause of primary hypogonadism.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**The story of development:**

During meiosis, nondisjunction of the X chromosome in either the egg (60% maternal) or sperm (40% paternal) results in a sperm or egg carrying an extra X. When fertilisation occurs, the zygote has 47 chromosomes (XXY) instead of 46. The extra X is inactivated (Barr body) but a few genes escape silencing – particularly those on the pseudoautosomal regions and the X-linked short stature homeobox (SHOX) gene.

**Why each key sign happens:**

- **Testicular failure** → The extra X disrupts Sertoli cell differentiation and Leydig cell function. By puberty, seminiferous tubules hyalinise and become fibrotic → **small firm testes** (the pathognomonic feel). Sertoli cells fail to produce inhibin B → loss of negative feedback → **elevated FSH**. Leydig cells produce inadequate testosterone → **elevated LH** (compensatory). - **Tall stature and eunuchoid proportions** → SHOX gene overexpression (from two X chromosomes) accelerates linear growth. Low oestrogen (from low T conversion) delays epiphyseal fusion → legs keep growing. Arm span > height, and pubis-to-floor > crown-to-pubis.

- **Learning difficulties** → The extra X affects brain regions responsible for language, verbal processing, and executive function. Patients often have dyslexia, impaired auditory processing, and reduced IQ (~10–15 points below siblings, but wide variability). Motor coordination is also affected.

- **Low bone mineral density** → Testosterone deficiency reduces osteoblastic activity → osteoporosis risk. Delayed puberty also reduces peak bone mass.

The classic vignette’s “small testes” and “high FSH” are direct consequences of Sertoli cell hyalinisation. The “learning difficulty” is a cortical effect of extra X. The “tall stature” is SHOX-driven. Each clue maps to a specific mechanistic step.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|---|---|---|
| **Chromosomal (most common)** | Nondisjunction during meiosis (paternal or maternal) | “Tall male with small testes; no family history” |
| **Mosaicism** | Post-zygotic mitotic error → 46,XY/47,XXY | “Less severe phenotype; may have normal testicular size” |
| **Additional X (rare)** | 48,XXXY or 49,XXXXY (increasing X count) | “More severe intellectual disability, dysmorphic face, radio-ulnar synostosis” |
| **Maternal age effect** | Slightly increased risk with older mothers | “Mother aged >40” (only 20% of cases, small effect) |

**Most common cause:** Sporadic nondisjunction in meiosis (no known trigger). No preventive strategy.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic confirmation:**

| Investigation | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Karyotype (peripheral blood)** | 47,XXY (or variant) | **Gold standard** – definitive diagnosis | One extra X in at least 10 cells; mosaic patterns show two cell lines. *Single finding confirms.* |

**Rule-out threshold:**  
If FSH and LH are normal and testicular volume > 12 ml, Klinefelter is very unlikely (except mosaics). Always check karyotype in infertile men with small testes regardless of gonadotropins.

3. **Refer to endocrinology** and **fertility specialist** (if desired) – discuss microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) success rate ~50% in non-mosaic adults; consider cryopreservation if any sperm found.

- **Ignoring learning disability** – may lead to school dropout; early educational support improves outcomes.

- **Assuming all tall, thin men have Klinefelter** – Marfan, homocystinuria, and eunuchoid habitus due to other hypogonadism should be excluded.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Klinefelter Syndrome | Kallmann Syndrome | Noonan Syndrome | Constitutional Delay of Puberty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pain | None | None | None | None |
| Exam | Tall, small testes, gynecomastia, eunuchoid | Anosmia, micropenis, cryptorchidism | Short stature, webbed neck, pectus excavatum, wide-spaced nipples | Short stature (normal height for bone age), small testes but normal for prepubertal |
| ECG | Normal | Normal | Often RVH (pulmonic stenosis) | Normal |
| Labs | High FSH/LH, low T, azoospermia | Low FSH/LH, low T (hypogonadotropic) | Normal FSH/LH (or mildly low), normal T for age | Low FSH/LH, low T (but appropriate for prepubertal stage) |

**Untreated Klinefelter syndrome** has a cascade of late effects. The stems that test “what happens if untreated” often describe a man in his 30s–40s with unexplained fractures, diabetes, and a history of “never hitting puberty”.

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Osteoporosis** | Low-trauma fracture (hip, vertebral) | Bone density T-score < -2.5; short height loss | DEXA scan; start TRT + calcium/vitamin D; consider bisphosphonates if severe |
| **Metabolic syndrome / type 2 diabetes** | Obesity, hypertension, dyslipidaemia at age 30–40 | High BMI, high triglycerides, low HDL, impaired fasting glucose | Metformin 500 mg BD titrated; lifestyle; TRT improves insulin sensitivity |

**Late-presentation pattern (the “missed” patient):**

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **Klinefelter: eXtra X, eXtra tall, eXtra small testes** – the three “X” clues.

**Five high-yield clinical pearls:**

1. **If you see tall + small testes + learning difficulty, think Klinefelter before Marfan.** Marfan has arachnodactyly, lens dislocation, and normal testes.
2. **Normal testicular volume > 12 ml essentially rules out classic Klinefelter.** But mosaics can have normal volume – check karyotype in any infertile man.
3. **TRT does NOT restore fertility.** In fact, it suppresses any residual spermatogenesis. If fertility is desired, offer sperm retrieval before starting TRT, or store sperm if present.
4. **Breast cancer in a male?** Check for Klinefelter – the relative risk is 20-fold. All men with gynaecomastia should have a breast exam and mammogram if asymmetric or firm.
5. **Klinefelter patients have a higher risk of autoimmune diseases** (SLE, RA, type 1 diabetes) – possibly due to altered X-inactivation and immune dysregulation.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Attributing high FSH/LH to “testicular damage from mumps” – always karyotype.
- Not ordering karyotype in azoospermic men – it’s the single most cost-effective test.
- Starting oral testosterone (e.g., Testosterone undecanoate capsules) – poor bioavailability and hepatotoxic.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

Klinefelter syndrome itself is rarely an acute emergency. However, complications or TRT adverse effects can require escalation:

| Scenario | Threshold | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Suspected VTE (DVT/PE)** | Sudden onset leg swelling/pain, dyspnoea, hypoxia, haemoptysis | Call MET / crash team if PE with hypotension; start LMWH; check haematocrit |
| **Haematocrit > 0.54** on TRT | Asymptomatic or symptomatic (headache, confusion) | Pause TRT; consider phlebotomy if haematocrit > 0.56; review dose reduction |
| **Psychiatric crisis** | Suicidal ideation, severe depression, acute anxiety | Call liaison psychiatry; consider hospitalisation; reassess TRT adequacy |
| **Hypoglycaemia / diabetic ketoacidosis** | If known diabetes, vomiting, abdominal pain, high ketones | Call medical registrar; manage per DKA protocol |
| **GCS drop** | Traumatic fracture with possible hypercalcaemia (immobilisation + osteoporosis) | CT head; calcium levels; admit for monitoring |

**General escalation:**  
- NEWS2 score ≥ 5 → urgent medical review.  
- NEWS2 score ≥ 7 → immediate call to MET/crash team.  
- Any new focal neurological sign, seizure, or loss of consciousness → emergency CT and endocrine review.
